agent_output:
{
  "domain_analysis": {
    "business_context": "A global logistics platform adding real-time driver earnings transparency across three jurisdictions with fundamentally different labor classifications, legal requirements, and governance structures. This is not a single feature — it is three jurisdiction-specific implementations sharing a common UI concept but governed by incompatible regulatory regimes.",
    "bounded_context": "Driver Compensation / Earnings Visibility",
    "ubiquitous_language": [
      { "term": "Base Pay", "definition": "The fixed compensation component for a delivery, before bonuses, tips, or deductions. For employees (CA), this must meet or exceed minimum wage on a per-hour basis. For contractors (Indonesia), this is the agreed platform rate." },
      { "term": "Per-Delivery Bonus", "definition": "Variable compensation tied to completing a specific delivery, such as surge pricing, distance bonuses, or batch incentives." },
      { "term": "Tip", "definition": "Voluntary gratuity from the customer to the driver. Legally distinct from wages in all three jurisdictions — must never be commingled with base pay calculations." },
      { "term": "Deduction", "definition": "Any amount subtracted from gross earnings: equipment fees, insurance contributions, tax withholdings, platform fees, or penalties. The legality, disclosure requirements, and driver-consent rules for deductions vary by jurisdiction." },
      { "term": "Earnings Breakdown", "definition": "An itemized view of all compensation components (base pay, bonuses, tips, deductions) for a given period, presented to the driver in real time." },
      { "term": "Driver Classification", "definition": "The legal relationship between driver and platform — employee (CA under AB5, Germany) or independent contractor (Indonesia). Determines which labor laws, tax rules, and transparency obligations apply." },
      { "term": "Works Council (Betriebsrat)", "definition": "A legally mandated employee representative body in Germany with co-determination rights. Must formally approve changes to compensation display systems before they go live, per the Betriebsverfassungsgesetz (Works Constitution Act)." },
      { "term": "Real Time", "definition": "Earnings data updated and visible to the driver within minutes of a delivery event completing — not batch/daily. Exact latency SLA to be defined per jurisdiction." },
      { "term": "Wage Transparency", "definition": "Legal obligation to disclose compensation structure and components to workers. Mandatory in California; governed by works council in Germany; not legally required in Indonesia." },
      { "term": "AB5", "definition": "California Assembly Bill 5 — codifies the ABC test for worker classification. Drivers operating in CA are employees, triggering minimum wage, overtime, and pay transparency obligations." }
    ]
  },

  "business_rules": [
    {
      "id": "BR-1",
      "rule": "Tips must never be counted toward base pay or minimum wage calculations in any jurisdiction.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Commingling tips with base pay is illegal in California (Labor Code §351), violates German wage law, and constitutes fraud against drivers in any jurisdiction. If violated, the system is producing legally and financially corrupt earnings data.",
      "testable_assertion": "For any earnings breakdown record, assert that the minimum wage compliance calculation for base pay excludes all tip amounts."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-2",
      "rule": "Every deduction displayed to a driver must have a named category, a legal basis, and a reference to driver consent or statutory authority.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Unexplained or unauthorized deductions constitute wage theft (CA), violate works council agreements (DE), or breach contractor agreements (ID). An unexplained deduction corrupts the integrity of the earnings record.",
      "testable_assertion": "For every deduction line item in an earnings breakdown, assert that category is non-empty, legal_basis is non-empty, and either driver_consent_timestamp or statutory_authority_reference is present."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-3",
      "rule": "In California, the earnings breakdown must be available to the driver for every pay period and must include all components required by California Labor Code §226 (gross wages, total hours, piece rates, deductions, net wages, pay period dates, employer identity).",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "California Labor Code §226 violations carry statutory penalties per employee per pay period. An incomplete pay stub is not just a UX gap — it is a legal violation that exposes the platform to class-action liability.",
      "testable_assertion": "For any California driver's earnings breakdown, assert presence of: gross_wages, total_hours_worked, all_piece_rates, itemized_deductions, net_wages, pay_period_start, pay_period_end, employer_legal_name, employer_address."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-4",
      "rule": "The Germany earnings display feature must not be activated for any German driver until a works council approval record exists with status 'approved' and an approval date.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Deploying compensation display changes without Betriebsrat approval violates the Works Constitution Act (§87 BetrVG) and can result in injunctions halting the feature entirely, plus back-pay claims. This is a hard legal gate, not a preference.",
      "testable_assertion": "Assert that the feature flag for Germany earnings display cannot be set to 'enabled' unless a works_council_approval record exists with status='approved' and approval_date is not null."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-5",
      "rule": "Earnings data must be displayed in the driver's local currency and the jurisdiction's official language(s), with amounts formatted per local convention.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Displaying earnings in the wrong currency or language renders the transparency feature meaningless and potentially non-compliant. A California driver seeing IDR or a German driver seeing English-only violates the purpose of the feature and local regulations.",
      "testable_assertion": "For each earnings breakdown, assert currency matches driver's jurisdiction (USD for CA, EUR for DE, IDR for ID) and language matches jurisdiction default."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-6",
      "rule": "For California employee-drivers, the system must track and display total hours worked alongside earnings to enable minimum wage and overtime verification.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Under AB5, California drivers are employees. Without hours tracking, neither the driver nor the platform can verify minimum wage compliance. This is a data integrity requirement — the earnings breakdown without hours is legally incomplete.",
      "testable_assertion": "For any California driver earnings breakdown, assert that total_hours_worked is present, is greater than zero, and that (base_pay / total_hours_worked) >= current California minimum wage."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-7",
      "rule": "For Indonesian contractor-drivers, the earnings breakdown must not display fields that imply an employment relationship (e.g., 'hours worked', 'overtime', 'employer').",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is a risk-mitigation policy to avoid reclassification arguments, not a data integrity constraint. A VP could decide to show hours for transparency purposes if legal counsel approved.",
      "testable_assertion": "For any Indonesian driver earnings breakdown, assert that the fields 'hours_worked', 'overtime_pay', and 'employer_name' are absent from the response payload."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-8",
      "rule": "The sum of (base_pay + bonuses + tips - deductions) must equal the displayed net_earnings for every breakdown record.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "If the arithmetic doesn't balance, the system is displaying corrupt financial data. This is a fundamental data integrity constraint.",
      "testable_assertion": "For every earnings breakdown record, assert that base_pay + sum(bonuses) + sum(tips) - sum(deductions) == net_earnings, within a rounding tolerance of 0.01 in local currency."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-9",
      "rule": "Earnings data must reflect completed delivery events only — pending, disputed, or cancelled deliveries must not appear in the confirmed earnings breakdown.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Showing unconfirmed earnings as confirmed is financially misleading and could constitute a deceptive practice. Drivers making financial decisions based on phantom earnings leads to real harm.",
      "testable_assertion": "For every line item in an earnings breakdown, assert that the associated delivery has status='completed' and payment_status='confirmed'."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-10",
      "rule": "In Germany, the earnings breakdown must comply with GDPR data minimization — display only compensation data necessary for transparency, and do not expose internal scoring, ranking, or algorithmic inputs.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "GDPR data minimization is a legal requirement, but the exact boundary of 'necessary' data is a policy judgment that could be refined with legal counsel or DPA guidance. The principle is invariant; the specific field list is policy.",
      "testable_assertion": "For any German driver earnings breakdown, assert that no fields related to driver_score, ranking_position, or algorithm_weight are present in the response."
    }
  ],

  "acceptance_criteria": [
    {
      "id": "AC-1",
      "given": "A California driver has completed 3 deliveries today",
      "when": "The driver opens the earnings breakdown screen",
      "then": "They see an itemized list of all 3 deliveries with base pay, bonuses, tips, and deductions for each, plus totals, total hours worked, and all fields required by CA Labor Code §226, in USD and English"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-2",
      "given": "The Germany works council has NOT approved the earnings display feature",
      "when": "A product manager attempts to enable the feature for German drivers",
      "then": "The system rejects the activation and returns an error referencing the missing works council approval"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-3",
      "given": "The Germany works council has approved the feature with approval date 2026-03-01",
      "when": "A German driver opens the earnings breakdown screen",
      "then": "They see their earnings breakdown in EUR, in German, with no internal scoring data exposed, and the feature activation audit log references the works council approval record"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-4",
      "given": "An Indonesian contractor-driver has completed 5 deliveries today",
      "when": "The driver opens the earnings breakdown screen",
      "then": "They see base pay, bonuses, tips, and platform fee deductions in IDR and Bahasa Indonesia, with no employment-implying fields (hours worked, overtime, employer name)"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-5",
      "given": "A driver in any jurisdiction has a delivery that is still in 'disputed' status",
      "when": "The driver views their earnings breakdown",
      "then": "The disputed delivery does NOT appear in the confirmed earnings section; it may appear in a separate 'pending/disputed' section clearly labeled as unconfirmed"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-6",
      "given": "A California driver's base pay for a period divided by hours worked falls below California minimum wage",
      "when": "The earnings breakdown is generated",
      "then": "The system flags the discrepancy for payroll review and does not suppress or hide the data from the driver"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-7",
      "given": "Any driver views their earnings breakdown",
      "when": "The net earnings figure is displayed",
      "then": "The arithmetic identity (base_pay + bonuses + tips - deductions = net_earnings) holds to within 0.01 local currency units"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-8",
      "given": "A driver completes a delivery with a customer tip",
      "when": "The earnings breakdown is generated",
      "then": "The tip appears as a separate line item and is excluded from any base pay or minimum wage calculation"
    }
  ],

  "edge_cases": [
    { "scenario": "A driver operates across jurisdictions (e.g., starts in one region, delivers in another)", "expected_behavior": "Earnings for each delivery are governed by the jurisdiction where the delivery was completed. The breakdown must not blend rules across jurisdictions within a single line item." },
    { "scenario": "Germany works council approval is revoked or expires after feature launch", "expected_behavior": "The feature must be immediately disabled for German drivers. The system must support approval revocation as a first-class event, not just initial approval." },
    { "scenario": "A customer modifies or removes a tip after it was already shown in the driver's breakdown", "expected_behavior": "The adjustment must appear as a separate, clearly labeled line item (e.g., 'tip adjustment') — never silently mutate a previously displayed record. The driver must see what changed and when." },
    { "scenario": "Platform fee deduction is introduced for Indonesian drivers mid-pay-period", "expected_behavior": "The new deduction applies only to deliveries completed after the policy effective date. Retroactive deductions require explicit driver consent per the contractor agreement." },
    { "scenario": "A California driver works a shift with zero deliveries completed but was logged in and available", "expected_behavior": "Under AB5 employee classification, the driver may be owed minimum wage for hours worked even without deliveries. The earnings breakdown must show hours worked with zero delivery earnings, not hide the period entirely." },
    { "scenario": "Network outage prevents real-time earnings update", "expected_behavior": "The system must clearly indicate that displayed data may be stale (e.g., 'last updated: 15 minutes ago') rather than showing potentially incomplete data as current. Must never display partial earnings as final." },
    { "scenario": "Currency conversion is needed (e.g., driver earns a cross-border delivery bonus denominated in a different currency)", "expected_behavior": "Display in the driver's local currency with the conversion rate and original amount shown. The conversion must use the rate at the time of the transaction, not the display time." },
    { "scenario": "A German driver requests deletion of their earnings data under GDPR right to erasure", "expected_behavior": "Earnings records are subject to statutory retention periods (German tax law requires 10-year retention of payroll records). The GDPR erasure request must be denied for these records with a clear legal basis citation." }
  ],

  "business_impact": {
    "financial": "High. California non-compliance with Labor Code §226 carries penalties of $50-$100+ per employee per pay period, scalable to class-action exposure. Germany rollout without works council approval can trigger injunctions and back-pay claims. Correct implementation builds driver trust, reducing churn — driver acquisition costs in logistics typically run $300-$1,500 per driver.",
    "operational": "Significant complexity. Three jurisdiction-specific implementations must be maintained in parallel, each with different field sets, legal gates, and update cadences. The works council approval gate in Germany introduces a non-engineering dependency that can block deployment indefinitely. Payroll, legal, and compliance teams become ongoing stakeholders.",
    "user": "Directly impacts driver trust, retention, and satisfaction. Transparent earnings breakdowns are consistently the #1 requested feature among gig/logistics drivers. Done right, this is a competitive differentiator. Done wrong (incorrect math, missing deductions, stale data), it actively destroys trust faster than having no feature at all.",
    "risk": "Multi-dimensional legal risk across three jurisdictions. California: wage-and-hour class actions. Germany: works council injunctions and co-determination disputes. Indonesia: reclassification risk if earnings display implies employment. Additionally, displaying real-time financial data creates a new attack surface for data integrity and privacy breaches.",
    "stakeholders_affected": [
      { "group": "Drivers (all jurisdictions)", "impact": "Primary users. Gain visibility into earnings, enabling them to make informed decisions about when and where to work. Incorrect data directly harms their financial planning and trust." },
      { "group": "Legal / Compliance Team", "impact": "Must validate jurisdiction-specific requirements before each rollout. Ongoing obligation to monitor regulatory changes and update the feature accordingly." },
      { "group": "Germany Works Council (Betriebsrat)", "impact": "Has co-determination rights over this feature. Must be engaged early, given review time, and may request modifications. Can block the Germany rollout entirely." },
      { "group": "Payroll / Finance", "impact": "The earnings breakdown is effectively a driver-facing payroll mirror. Any discrepancy between what payroll calculates and what the driver sees creates disputes, reconciliation burden, and audit risk." },
      { "group": "Product / Engineering", "impact": "Must build and maintain three jurisdiction-specific variants, handle feature-flagging by region, and manage the works council approval workflow as a system dependency." },
      { "group": "Customers", "impact": "Indirect impact. Tip transparency may influence tipping behavior. Customers in some jurisdictions may see that their tip is displayed separately to drivers, which could be positive or negative for tipping rates." }
    ]
  },

  "confidence": {
    "level": 45,
    "reasoning": "The spec is a single sentence ('show each driver their earnings breakdown in real time') spanning three jurisdictions with fundamentally different legal regimes. Critical details are missing: exact fields required per jurisdiction, the platform's existing payroll system architecture, whether works council engagement has begun in Germany, the contractor agreement terms in Indonesia, and whether 'real time' means per-delivery or per-minute. The legal requirements cited (AB5, §226, BetrVG §87, GDPR) are well-established, but their specific application to THIS platform's driver model requires legal review that has not been referenced. The single-sentence spec dramatically understates the complexity.",
    "high_confidence_areas": [
      "Tips must not be commingled with base pay — universally accepted across all three jurisdictions",
      "Works council approval is a hard gate for Germany — this is black-letter law, not interpretation",
      "California drivers under AB5 are employees and subject to §226 pay stub requirements",
      "Earnings arithmetic must balance — fundamental data integrity",
      "Indonesia contractor classification means avoiding employment-implying language"
    ],
    "low_confidence_areas": [
      "Exact field-level requirements for California §226 compliance for this specific platform model",
      "Whether the Germany works council has been engaged or what their likely objections will be",
      "Indonesian contractor agreement terms and what deductions are permitted",
      "What 'real time' means operationally — latency SLA is undefined",
      "Whether cross-jurisdiction driver scenarios exist and how common they are",
      "Tax withholding display requirements per jurisdiction"
    ],
    "assumptions": [
      "The platform has an existing payroll system that is the source of truth for earnings calculations — this feature is a display/transparency layer, not the calculation engine",
      "California drivers are correctly classified as employees under AB5 and the platform is already compliant with basic wage-and-hour law",
      "The Germany operation has an established works council — if no works council exists, the approval gate rule changes significantly",
      "Indonesian drivers have signed contractor agreements that define permitted platform fees and deductions",
      "The platform already tracks delivery completion events with timestamps sufficient to derive earnings in near-real-time"
    ]
  },

  "escalations": [
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "The spec says 'real time' but does not define latency requirements. Real-time per-delivery (within seconds of completion), real-time per-shift (running totals updated every few minutes), or real-time per-pay-period (always-current period view) are fundamentally different features with different engineering and legal implications. California §226 requires pay stub delivery at time of payment, not necessarily real-time — so 'real time' may exceed legal requirements but introduce data consistency risks.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Drivers", "Engineering", "Payroll"],
      "options": ["A: Per-delivery real-time (highest engineering cost, best UX)", "B: Per-shift running totals (moderate cost, good UX)", "C: Per-pay-period always-current view (lowest cost, meets legal minimum)"],
      "recommendation": "B — per-shift running totals balance driver value with engineering feasibility and reduce the risk of showing premature/unconfirmed data"
    },
    {
      "type": "missing_context",
      "description": "No legal review has been referenced for any of the three jurisdictions. The business rules above are based on general legal knowledge, not jurisdiction-specific legal counsel for THIS platform. California §226 requirements depend on the specific employment structure. German works council requirements depend on whether a Betriebsrat exists and the scope of existing agreements. Indonesian rules depend on the specific contractor agreement terms.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Legal / Compliance", "All drivers", "Executive leadership"],
      "options": ["A: Proceed with domain analysis as baseline, require legal sign-off before implementation", "B: Block all work until legal review is complete"],
      "recommendation": "A — use this analysis as a starting framework but make legal review a hard gate before any jurisdiction goes live. Legal sign-off should be a deployment prerequisite, similar to the works council gate for Germany."
    },
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "The spec lists 'deductions' as a category but does not enumerate what deductions exist on this platform. Deductions for employee-drivers (tax withholding, benefits, insurance) are fundamentally different from contractor deductions (platform fees, equipment rental). Without knowing the platform's deduction taxonomy, acceptance criteria for deduction display are necessarily incomplete.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Drivers", "Payroll / Finance", "Legal"],
      "options": ["A: Define a generic deduction model with category and legal basis fields, let each jurisdiction populate", "B: Enumerate all known deduction types before building"],
      "recommendation": "B — enumerate first. A generic model risks missing jurisdiction-specific requirements (e.g., California requires itemized deductions, not just a lump sum)."
    }
  ],

  "rejection_reasons": []
}
